items.q: Which past tense sets the scene in a holiday account?
items.correct: The imperfect describes background and ongoing situations (hacía sol, había mucha gente), while the preterite reports completed events (visité, fui).
items.misconception: The preterite is used for everything that happened in the past.
items.random_wrong: Spanish has only one past tense, like English.
items.why: Choosing between the two past tenses is a key GCSE skill; using the preterite for descriptions flattens the narrative and loses accuracy marks.

items.q: What is 'un plano' at the tourist office?
items.correct: A street map of the city.
items.misconception: A plane, because of the similar spelling.
items.random_wrong: A plant for your hotel room.
items.why: 'Plane' is 'el avión'; 'plano' (map) versus 'plan' versus 'avión' confusions regularly derail role-play and reading tasks about travel.

items.q: What is the difference between 'la playa' and 'la plaza'?
items.correct: La playa is the beach; la plaza is the town square.
items.misconception: They are two spellings of the same word for beach.
items.random_wrong: La plaza means the swimming pool.
items.why: One letter changes the location entirely; listening tasks exploit this minimal pair, so learners must hear and spell both accurately.

items.q: 'Tomé el sol en la playa' means what?
items.misconception: I drank juice on the beach
items.random_wrong: I took photos of the beach
items.why: The answer examiners want is: I sunbathed on the beach. “I drank juice on the beach” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Why is the imperfect used in 'Hacía sol todos los días'?
items.correct: It describes ongoing or repeated weather in the past
items.why: It's It describes ongoing or repeated weather in the past. “It refers to the future” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: 'El albergue juvenil' is what kind of accommodation?
items.why: It's A youth hostel. “A luxury hotel” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: 'Mi ciudad está situada en el norte' means what?
items.correct: My town is located in the north
items.misconception: My town is sitting on a mountain
items.random_wrong: My town used to be in the north
items.why: Correct answer: My town is located in the north. “My town is sitting on a mountain” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
